1:
"And it shall come to pass, when all these things are come upon thee, the blessing and the curse, which I have set before thee, and thou shalt call them to mind among all the nations..." The witnesses note the foretold day when, the blessing and curse fulfilled, Israel in their dispersion would call them to mind. The witnesses note that Gill notes these things were declared, foretold, and prophesied in the three preceding chapters. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the blessing and the curse come upon them, called to mind among the nations. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all these things — the blessing and the curse fulfilled; the among the nations — in the dispersion; the call them to mind — the judgment working reflection; the mercy beyond the curse — the rod awakening return (Hos. 5:15).

The witnesses agree the chapter opens with a gracious turn after the threatened curses: when "all these things... the blessing and the curse" had befallen them and they were scattered, they would "call them to mind" — the judgment itself working remembrance and reflection; the foretelling of this shows God's mercy reaching beyond the curse, providing for a recovery through the very chastisement; it teaches that God's afflictions are meant to bring his people to themselves and to him, the rod awakening the conscience, "I will go and return to my place, till they acknowledge their offence" (Hos. 5:15), the scattering the prelude to a return.

So the witnesses make the verse call them to mind: The witnesses note the all these things: the blessing and the curse fulfilled. The witnesses note the among the nations: in the dispersion. The witnesses note the call them to mind: the judgment working reflection. The witnesses note the mercy beyond the curse: the rod awakening return (Hos. 5:15). The expositors observe the all these things: the blessing and the curse fulfilled. The expositors observe the among the nations: in the dispersion. The expositors observe the call them to mind: the judgment working reflection. The expositors observe the mercy beyond the curse: the rod awakening return (Hos. 5:15). The believer is taught to mark the all these things, the among the nations, the call them to mind, the mercy beyond the curse, and to return unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that the scattered people would call the blessing and curse to mind — God's mercy reaching beyond the curse, the chastisement working remembrance, the scattering the prelude to a return (Hos. 5:15).
2:
"And shalt return unto the LORD thy God, and shalt obey his voice according to all that I command thee this day, thou and thy children, with all thine heart, and with all thy soul;" The witnesses note the promised repentance — Israel returning to the LORD and obeying with all the heart. The witnesses note that Gill notes the returning by repentance, acknowledging their manifold sins, particularly their rejection of the Messiah, now seeking him and salvation by him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the returning and obeying with all the heart and soul. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the return unto the LORD — by true repentance; the obey his voice — seeking salvation in him; the with all thine heart — a whole, sincere turning; the mercy to the returning — let him return, and have mercy (Isa. 55:7).

The witnesses agree the turning-point of the whole passage is a true and whole-hearted repentance: Israel would "return unto the LORD" and obey "with all thine heart, and with all thy soul"; Gill reads it of the future conversion of the Jews, their acknowledging their sin, especially the rejection of the Messiah, and seeking salvation in him; the verse marks repentance as the way back to God after the curse — a sincere, entire turning of the heart; it teaches that the door of mercy stands open to the returning sinner, "let the wicked forsake his way... and let him return unto the LORD, and he will have mercy" (Isa. 55:7), the whole heart the measure of true repentance.

So the witnesses make the verse return unto the LORD: The witnesses note the return unto the LORD: by true repentance. The witnesses note the obey his voice: seeking salvation in him. The witnesses note the with all thine heart: a whole, sincere turning. The witnesses note the mercy to the returning: let him return, and have mercy (Isa. 55:7). The expositors observe the return unto the LORD: by true repentance. The expositors observe the obey his voice: seeking salvation in him. The expositors observe the with all thine heart: a whole, sincere turning. The expositors observe the mercy to the returning: let him return, and have mercy (Isa. 55:7). The believer is taught to mark the return unto the LORD, the obey his voice, the with all thine heart, the mercy to the returning, and to return unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that Israel would return to the LORD and obey with all the heart — repentance the way back after the curse, the door of mercy open to the returning, the whole heart the measure of true repentance (Isa. 55:7).
3:
"That then the LORD thy God will turn thy captivity, and have compassion upon thee, and will return and gather thee from all the nations, whither the LORD thy God hath scattered thee." The witnesses note God's promise to turn their captivity and gather them again in compassion. The witnesses note that Gill notes the turning of their captivity both temporally and spiritually, freeing them from exile and from the bondage of sin, Satan, and the law, as the effect of his grace. The witnesses note that he would gather them from all the nations. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the turn thy captivity — the restoring mercy; the have compassion — a Father's heart; the gather thee — from all the nations; the the freeness of welcome — the father had compassion (Luke 15:20).

The witnesses agree the answer to repentance is God's tender restoring mercy: when they returned, "then the LORD... will turn thy captivity, and have compassion," gathering the scattered from all nations; Gill reads it of both the temporal regathering and the spiritual deliverance from the bondage of sin; the verse shows that God meets the returning sinner with compassion and full restoration, not grudgingly but with a Father's heart; it teaches the freeness of his welcome, "while he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion" (Luke 15:20), the captivity turned and the wanderer gathered home.

So the witnesses make the verse turn the captivity: The witnesses note the turn thy captivity: the restoring mercy. The witnesses note the have compassion: a Father's heart. The witnesses note the gather thee: from all the nations. The witnesses note the the freeness of welcome: the father had compassion (Luke 15:20). The expositors observe the turn thy captivity: the restoring mercy. The expositors observe the have compassion: a Father's heart. The expositors observe the gather thee: from all the nations. The expositors observe the the freeness of welcome: the father had compassion (Luke 15:20). The believer is taught to mark the turn thy captivity, the have compassion, the gather thee, the the freeness of welcome, and to return unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that God would turn their captivity and gather them in compassion — his tender restoring mercy meeting the returning, the captivity turned and the wanderer gathered home with a Father's heart (Luke 15:20).
4:
"If any of thine be driven out unto the outmost parts of heaven, from thence will the LORD thy God gather thee, and from thence will he fetch thee:" The witnesses note that even the farthest-scattered would be gathered — none beyond God's reach. The witnesses note that Gill notes that some are driven to the outmost parts, even to remote islands and lands, yet thence the Lord would gather them. The witnesses note that from thence he would fetch them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the outmost parts of heaven — the farthest scattered; the gather thee — none beyond reach; the fetch thee thence — from the most distant; the no wanderer too far — the uttermost gathered (Neh. 1:9).

The witnesses agree the promise reaches to the most distant of the scattered, that none be beyond recovery: though driven "unto the outmost parts of heaven," the LORD would "gather" and "fetch" them thence; the extremity of the dispersion is matched by the extent of the gathering, no place too far for God's saving hand; it teaches that no sinner is so far gone, no wanderer so distant, but God can reach and restore him, "though there were of you cast out unto the uttermost part of heaven, yet will I gather them" (Neh. 1:9), the farthest brought home.

So the witnesses make the verse gathered from afar: The witnesses note the outmost parts of heaven: the farthest scattered. The witnesses note the gather thee: none beyond reach. The witnesses note the fetch thee thence: from the most distant. The witnesses note the no wanderer too far: the uttermost gathered (Neh. 1:9). The expositors observe the outmost parts of heaven: the farthest scattered. The expositors observe the gather thee: none beyond reach. The expositors observe the fetch thee thence: from the most distant. The expositors observe the no wanderer too far: the uttermost gathered (Neh. 1:9). The believer is taught to mark the outmost parts of heaven, the gather thee, the fetch thee thence, the no wanderer too far, and to return unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that even the farthest-scattered would be gathered — none beyond God's reach, no wanderer too distant to be restored, the uttermost brought home (Neh. 1:9).
5:
"And the LORD thy God will bring thee into the land which thy fathers possessed, and thou shalt possess it; and he will do thee good, and multiply thee above thy fathers." The witnesses note the promise of restoration to the land, with good and increase beyond the fathers. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Jews upon their conversion in the latter day will return to the land of Judea and possess it, the sense of many passages of Scripture. The witnesses note that God would do them good and multiply them above their fathers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the bring thee into the land — the restoration; the thou shalt possess it — the inheritance regained; the do thee good — the abundance of mercy; the multiply above thy fathers — the latter greater than the former (Joel 2:25).

The witnesses agree the restoration would surpass their former estate: God would bring them back to the land, "do thee good, and multiply thee above thy fathers"; Gill reads it of the latter-day conversion and restoration of the Jews; the promise of an increase beyond the fathers shows the abundance of God's mercy in restoring, the recovery exceeding the loss; it teaches that God's grace not only restores the penitent but enriches them beyond their first estate, the latter glory greater than the former, "I will restore to you the years that the locust hath eaten" (Joel 2:25), the restored people multiplied.

So the witnesses make the verse restored and increased: The witnesses note the bring thee into the land: the restoration. The witnesses note the thou shalt possess it: the inheritance regained. The witnesses note the do thee good: the abundance of mercy. The witnesses note the multiply above thy fathers: the latter greater than the former (Joel 2:25). The expositors observe the bring thee into the land: the restoration. The expositors observe the thou shalt possess it: the inheritance regained. The expositors observe the do thee good: the abundance of mercy. The expositors observe the multiply above thy fathers: the latter greater than the former (Joel 2:25). The believer is taught to mark the bring thee into the land, the thou shalt possess it, the do thee good, the multiply above thy fathers, and to return unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that God would restore them to the land and multiply them above their fathers — the recovery exceeding the loss, his grace enriching the penitent beyond their first estate, the years restored (Joel 2:25).
6:
"And the LORD thy God will circumcise thine heart, and the heart of thy seed, to love the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy soul, that thou mayest live." The witnesses note the great promise — God himself circumcising the heart, that they may love him and live. The witnesses note that Gill notes no mention is made of circumcision of the flesh, now out of use; it is spiritual circumcision only, the work of God on the heart, the Jews receiving the Messiah and convinced of the abrogation of the ceremonial law. The witnesses note that this was to enable them to love God with all the heart. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the circumcise thine heart — spiritual, not of the flesh; the the LORD will do it — god's own work, not man's; the to love the LORD — the new heart that loves him; the the promise of grace — a new heart will I give (Ezek. 36:26; Rom. 2:29).

The witnesses agree this verse holds the heart of the gospel within the law — the promise of inward renewal wrought by God himself: "the LORD thy God will circumcise thine heart," the spiritual circumcision that the flesh's could only typify, that they might "love the LORD... with all thine heart"; the love commanded elsewhere is here promised as God's own gift, the new heart his work, not man's; it is the promise of regeneration, "a new heart also will I give you" (Ezek. 36:26), "the circumcision... of the heart, in the spirit" (Rom. 2:29), fulfilled in the new covenant — that God gives what he requires, working in his people both to will and to do.

So the witnesses make the verse circumcise the heart: The witnesses note the circumcise thine heart: spiritual, not of the flesh. The witnesses note the the LORD will do it: God's own work, not man's. The witnesses note the to love the LORD: the new heart that loves him. The witnesses note the the promise of grace: a new heart will I give (Ezek. 36:26; Rom. 2:29). The expositors observe the circumcise thine heart: spiritual, not of the flesh. The expositors observe the the LORD will do it: God's own work, not man's. The expositors observe the to love the LORD: the new heart that loves him. The expositors observe the the promise of grace: a new heart will I give (Ezek. 36:26; Rom. 2:29). The believer is taught to mark the circumcise thine heart, the the LORD will do it, the to love the LORD, the the promise of grace, and to return unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that God himself would circumcise the heart, that they might love him — the gospel within the law, the new heart his own work, the promise of regeneration that God gives what he requires (Ezek. 36:26; Rom. 2:29).
7:
"And the LORD thy God will put all these curses upon thine enemies, and on them that hate thee, which persecuted thee." The witnesses note the curses turned from the restored people upon their persecuting enemies. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Word of the Lord, now sought and embraced, will be their King and Saviour, and revenge their enemies. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the curses put upon their enemies. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all these curses — once on Israel; the upon thine enemies — the persecutors; the God their avenger — now their King and Saviour; the the tables turned — vengeance is mine (Rom. 12:19).

The witnesses agree that with the people's restoration came the turning of judgment upon their oppressors: the curses once on Israel would "fall upon thine enemies... which persecuted thee"; God, now sought and embraced as King and Saviour, would avenge his people on those who hated them; the verse shows that God takes up the cause of his restored ones against their foes, the tables turned when his mercy returns to them; it teaches that the God who chastens his own will yet plead their cause and repay their persecutors, "vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord" (Rom. 12:19), the enemies of the redeemed brought under the curse.

So the witnesses make the verse curses on the enemies: The witnesses note the all these curses: once on Israel. The witnesses note the upon thine enemies: the persecutors. The witnesses note the God their avenger: now their King and Saviour. The witnesses note the the tables turned: vengeance is mine (Rom. 12:19). The expositors observe the all these curses: once on Israel. The expositors observe the upon thine enemies: the persecutors. The expositors observe the God their avenger: now their King and Saviour. The expositors observe the the tables turned: vengeance is mine (Rom. 12:19). The believer is taught to mark the all these curses, the upon thine enemies, the God their avenger, the the tables turned, and to return unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that the curses would fall upon their persecuting enemies — God taking up the cause of his restored people, pleading their cause and repaying their foes, vengeance his (Rom. 12:19).
8:
"And thou shalt return and obey the voice of the LORD, and do all his commandments which I command thee this day." The witnesses note the renewed and willing obedience that follows the heart's renewal. The witnesses note that Gill notes the obeying of the Gospel, embracing its truth and yielding cheerful subjection to its ordinances, receiving the Word of the Lord, Christ, by faith. The witnesses note that they would do all his commandments. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thou shalt return — from the renewed heart; the obey the voice — willing service, not forced; the do all his commandments — the new walk; the grace producing obedience — god worketh in you to will and do (Phil. 2:13).

The witnesses agree the renewed obedience flows from the circumcised heart: having received the new heart, they "shall return and obey... and do all his commandments" — not the forced obedience of the old nature but the willing service of the renewed; Gill reads it of obeying the gospel and receiving Christ by faith; the verse shows that true obedience is the fruit of regeneration, the new heart producing the new walk; it teaches that God's working within issues in our working without, "for it is God which worketh in you both to will and to do" (Phil. 2:13), the renewed heart obeying from love.

So the witnesses make the verse renewed obedience: The witnesses note the thou shalt return: from the renewed heart. The witnesses note the obey the voice: willing service, not forced. The witnesses note the do all his commandments: the new walk. The witnesses note the grace producing obedience: God worketh in you to will and do (Phil. 2:13). The expositors observe the thou shalt return: from the renewed heart. The expositors observe the obey the voice: willing service, not forced. The expositors observe the do all his commandments: the new walk. The expositors observe the grace producing obedience: God worketh in you to will and do (Phil. 2:13). The believer is taught to mark the thou shalt return, the obey the voice, the do all his commandments, the grace producing obedience, and to return unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that they would return and obey all God's commandments — the willing obedience flowing from the circumcised heart, regeneration producing the new walk, God working in them to will and do (Phil. 2:13).
9:
"And the LORD thy God will make thee plenteous in every work of thine hand... for the LORD will again rejoice over thee for good, as he rejoiced over thy fathers:" The witnesses note the promise of plenty, and the LORD rejoicing over his restored people for good. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord would greatly bless them in all they set their hands to in a lawful way, so that they should abound and have enough and to spare. The witnesses note that the LORD would again rejoice over them for good. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the plenteous in every work — the abundant blessing; the again rejoice over thee — for good; the as over thy fathers — his renewed delight; the God's glad love — he will joy over thee with singing (Zeph. 3:17).

The witnesses agree the restored people are crowned with blessing and, more, with God's own joy: he would "make thee plenteous in every work" and "again rejoice over thee for good"; the abundance is great, but the greater wonder is that God should "rejoice over" them — delighting in his people as a bridegroom in his bride; the verse reveals the heart of God toward his own, not bare benefit but glad love; it teaches the tenderness of God's affection for the redeemed, "he will rejoice over thee with joy... he will joy over thee with singing" (Zeph. 3:17), the restored made the object of his delight.

So the witnesses make the verse God rejoices over thee: The witnesses note the plenteous in every work: the abundant blessing. The witnesses note the again rejoice over thee: for good. The witnesses note the as over thy fathers: his renewed delight. The witnesses note the God's glad love: he will joy over thee with singing (Zeph. 3:17). The expositors observe the plenteous in every work: the abundant blessing. The expositors observe the again rejoice over thee: for good. The expositors observe the as over thy fathers: his renewed delight. The expositors observe the God's glad love: he will joy over thee with singing (Zeph. 3:17). The believer is taught to mark the plenteous in every work, the again rejoice over thee, the as over thy fathers, the God's glad love, and to return unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that God would make them plenteous and rejoice over them for good — not bare benefit but glad love, the tenderness of God's delight in his restored people, joying over them with singing (Zeph. 3:17).
10:
"If thou shalt hearken unto the voice of the LORD thy God, to keep his commandments and his statutes... and if thou turn unto the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy soul." The witnesses note the blessing joined to whole-hearted obedience and turning — itself the fruit of the promised grace. The witnesses note that Gill notes it should rather be read "for thou shalt hearken," since it was before promised that God would circumcise their hearts to love him and they should obey his voice. The witnesses note that they would turn with all the heart and soul. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the hearken unto the voice — keeping his commandments; the turn with all thine heart — the whole-hearted turning; the rather, for thou shalt — the fruit of promised grace; the what he requires he gives — his fear put in the heart (Jer. 32:40).

The witnesses agree the condition of blessing is itself secured by the foregoing promise of grace: though it reads "if thou shalt hearken... and turn," Gill rightly notes it may be read "for thou shalt hearken," seeing God had promised to circumcise their hearts that they might obey; thus the very obedience required is the gift assured; it teaches the deep harmony of grace and duty, that what God commands he also bestows, the condition fulfilled by his own working — "I will put my fear in their hearts, that they shall not depart from me" (Jer. 32:40), the turning with all the heart both required and given.

So the witnesses make the verse thou shalt hearken: The witnesses note the hearken unto the voice: keeping his commandments. The witnesses note the turn with all thine heart: the whole-hearted turning. The witnesses note the rather, for thou shalt: the fruit of promised grace. The witnesses note the what he requires he gives: his fear put in the heart (Jer. 32:40). The expositors observe the hearken unto the voice: keeping his commandments. The expositors observe the turn with all thine heart: the whole-hearted turning. The expositors observe the rather, for thou shalt: the fruit of promised grace. The expositors observe the what he requires he gives: his fear put in the heart (Jer. 32:40). The believer is taught to mark the hearken unto the voice, the turn with all thine heart, the rather, for thou shalt, the what he requires he gives, and to return unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that the blessing is joined to whole-hearted obedience — itself the fruit of the promised grace, what God requires he also bestows, the turning with all the heart both required and given (Jer. 32:40).
11:
"For this commandment which I command thee this day, it is not hidden from thee, neither is it far off." The witnesses note that the commandment is neither too hard nor far off, but within reach. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Jews understand it of the law, but the Apostle Paul interprets it of the word of faith, the Gospel — which better suits the context and the prophecies of their conversion. The witnesses note that it is not hidden nor far off. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the this commandment — the word God gives; the not hidden from thee — plain and accessible; the neither far off — within reach; the the word of faith — the gospel brought near (Rom. 10:8).

The witnesses agree the word God gives is accessible, not beyond man's reach: "this commandment... is not hidden from thee, neither is it far off"; Gill notes the apostle Paul applies these very verses to "the word of faith" — the gospel (Rom. 10:8) — making the passage a promise of the nearness of salvation; the verse teaches that God has not set his way of life at an unreachable distance, but brought it near, the truth plain and accessible to all who will receive it, no hard ascent or far journey required to find it.

So the witnesses make the verse not hidden nor far: The witnesses note the this commandment: the word God gives. The witnesses note the not hidden from thee: plain and accessible. The witnesses note the neither far off: within reach. The witnesses note the the word of faith: the gospel brought near (Rom. 10:8). The expositors observe the this commandment: the word God gives. The expositors observe the not hidden from thee: plain and accessible. The expositors observe the neither far off: within reach. The expositors observe the the word of faith: the gospel brought near (Rom. 10:8). The believer is taught to mark the this commandment, the not hidden from thee, the neither far off, the the word of faith, and to choose life. The verse thus records that the commandment is not hidden nor far off — God's way of life accessible, the word brought near, applied by Paul to the word of faith, the gospel within reach of all (Rom. 10:8).
12:
"It is not in heaven, that thou shouldest say, Who shall go up for us to heaven, and bring it unto us, that we may hear it, and do it?" The witnesses note that the word is not in heaven, needing one to ascend and fetch it. The witnesses note that Gill notes the scheme of the gospel was formed in heaven and hid in God, but brought down from thence by Christ and committed to his ministers to preach to men. The witnesses note that none need go up to heaven for it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not in heaven — no ascent needed; the who shall go up — the impossible feat unrequired; the brought down by Christ — to his ministers; the no need to ascend — to bring Christ down (Rom. 10:6).

The witnesses agree the word is not so far above as to need a man's ascent to fetch it: "it is not in heaven... who shall go up for us?" — the truth has been brought down, not left beyond reach; Gill marks that what was hidden in heaven was "brought down... by Christ" and given to his ministers; the apostle reads this of Christ, that we need not ask "who shall ascend into heaven?... that is, to bring Christ down" (Rom. 10:6), for he is already come; it teaches that salvation is not to be earned by some impossible feat, but received as a gift already brought near, the labour of fetching it done by Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse not in heaven: The witnesses note the not in heaven: no ascent needed. The witnesses note the who shall go up: the impossible feat unrequired. The witnesses note the brought down by Christ: to his ministers. The witnesses note the no need to ascend: to bring Christ down (Rom. 10:6). The expositors observe the not in heaven: no ascent needed. The expositors observe the who shall go up: the impossible feat unrequired. The expositors observe the brought down by Christ: to his ministers. The expositors observe the no need to ascend: to bring Christ down (Rom. 10:6). The believer is taught to mark the not in heaven, the who shall go up, the brought down by Christ, the no need to ascend, and to choose life. The verse thus records that the word is not in heaven needing a man's ascent — the truth brought down by Christ, salvation not earned by some impossible feat but received as already near, no need to ascend to bring Christ down (Rom. 10:6).
13:
"Neither is it beyond the sea, that thou shouldest say, Who shall go over the sea for us, and bring it unto us, that we may hear it, and do it?" The witnesses note that the word is not beyond the sea, needing a far journey to obtain it. The witnesses note that Gill notes there is no need to travel into foreign or transmarine countries for it, as the heathen philosophers did for knowledge; the gospel is to be preached to all the world. The witnesses note that none need go over the sea for it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not beyond the sea — no far voyage needed; the who shall go over — the distant search unrequired; the preached to all the world — brought to men where they are; the the work finished — no need to bring Christ up (Rom. 10:7).

The witnesses agree the word requires no far voyage to find it: "neither is it beyond the sea... who shall go over for us?" — no need to journey to distant lands as the philosophers did in search of wisdom; the gospel is sent into all the world, brought to men where they are; the apostle likewise reads it of Christ, that none need ask "who shall descend into the deep?... to bring up Christ again from the dead" (Rom. 10:7), for the work is finished; it teaches that the truth of salvation is not hidden in some far place to be sought with great labour, but published freely and near, the seeking done by God who sends it.

So the witnesses make the verse not beyond the sea: The witnesses note the not beyond the sea: no far voyage needed. The witnesses note the who shall go over: the distant search unrequired. The witnesses note the preached to all the world: brought to men where they are. The witnesses note the the work finished: no need to bring Christ up (Rom. 10:7). The expositors observe the not beyond the sea: no far voyage needed. The expositors observe the who shall go over: the distant search unrequired. The expositors observe the preached to all the world: brought to men where they are. The expositors observe the the work finished: no need to bring Christ up (Rom. 10:7). The believer is taught to mark the not beyond the sea, the who shall go over, the preached to all the world, the the work finished, and to choose life. The verse thus records that the word is not beyond the sea needing a far journey — no distant search required, the gospel published freely and near, the work of bringing Christ finished by God who sends his word (Rom. 10:7).
14:
"But the word is very nigh unto thee, in thy mouth, and in thy heart, that thou mayest do it." The witnesses note the word brought very near — in the mouth and in the heart — that it may be done. The witnesses note that Gill notes the word nigh in its ministration by faithful preachers, and in its application by the Spirit to the heart, and in the gracious experience of converted persons. The witnesses note that Clarke likewise marks its nearness. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the very nigh unto thee — brought home; the in thy mouth — confessed; the in thy heart — believed and applied by the Spirit; the the word of faith — confess and believe, and be saved (Rom. 10:8-9).

The witnesses agree this verse crowns the passage with the nearness of the word and the saving truth: "the word is very nigh unto thee, in thy mouth, and in thy heart" — brought home by faithful preaching and the Spirit's inward application; the apostle takes it up as "the word of faith, which we preach," and adds, "if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart... thou shalt be saved" (Rom. 10:8-9); it teaches that the gospel is no distant or difficult thing but near, in the mouth that confesses and the heart that believes, the way of salvation laid close at hand for faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the word very nigh: The witnesses note the very nigh unto thee: brought home. The witnesses note the in thy mouth: confessed. The witnesses note the in thy heart: believed and applied by the Spirit. The witnesses note the the word of faith: confess and believe, and be saved (Rom. 10:8-9). The expositors observe the very nigh unto thee: brought home. The expositors observe the in thy mouth: confessed. The expositors observe the in thy heart: believed and applied by the Spirit. The expositors observe the the word of faith: confess and believe, and be saved (Rom. 10:8-9). The believer is taught to mark the very nigh unto thee, the in thy mouth, the in thy heart, the the word of faith, and to choose life. The verse thus records that the word is very nigh, in the mouth and heart, that it may be done — the saving truth brought home by preaching and the Spirit, the word of faith near in the mouth that confesses and the heart that believes (Rom. 10:8-9).
15:
"See, I have set before thee this day life and good, and death and evil;" The witnesses note Moses setting before them the great choice — life and good, or death and evil. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses returns to press the present observance of the laws, as of great moment. The witnesses note that Henry marks Moses concluding with a very bright light, setting life and death before them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I have set before thee — the great alternative; the life and good — the way of obedience; the death and evil — the way of sin; the the weight of choice — life or death set before all (Rom. 6:23).

The witnesses agree the verse opens the great and solemn alternative set before every soul: "life and good, and death and evil" — the two ways and the two ends, plainly placed before them for choice; Henry marks the brightness with which Moses closes, pressing the momentous decision; the setting of life and death before the people shows that God deals with men as responsible beings, calling them to choose, and that obedience is the way of life and sin the way of death; it teaches the weight of the choice laid on every hearer, the same alternative the gospel presses, "the gift of God is eternal life... but the wages of sin is death" (Rom. 6:23).

So the witnesses make the verse life and death set forth: The witnesses note the I have set before thee: the great alternative. The witnesses note the life and good: the way of obedience. The witnesses note the death and evil: the way of sin. The witnesses note the the weight of choice: life or death set before all (Rom. 6:23). The expositors observe the I have set before thee: the great alternative. The expositors observe the life and good: the way of obedience. The expositors observe the death and evil: the way of sin. The expositors observe the the weight of choice: life or death set before all (Rom. 6:23). The believer is taught to mark the I have set before thee, the life and good, the death and evil, the the weight of choice, and to choose life. The verse thus records that Moses set before them life and good, death and evil — the great alternative laid before every soul, obedience the way of life and sin the way of death, the choice the gospel still presses (Rom. 6:23).
16:
"In that I command thee this day to love the LORD thy God, to walk in his ways... that thou mayest live and multiply: and the LORD thy God shall bless thee..." The witnesses note the way of life — to love God, walk in his ways, and keep his commandments. The witnesses note that Gill notes the loving of the Lord is the sum of the first table of the law, the contrary being idolatry and will-worship. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the loving, walking, and keeping that brings life and blessing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the to love the LORD — the sum of the first table; the walk in his ways — the obedient walk; the keep his commandments — the spring of love; the life and blessing — if ye love me, keep my commandments (John 14:15).

The witnesses agree the way of "life and good" is defined as love and obedience to God: "to love the LORD thy God, to walk in his ways, and to keep his commandments"; the love of God, Gill notes, is the sum of the first table, the spring of all true obedience; to this God joins the promise of life, increase, and blessing; the verse teaches that the chosen way of life is a walk of love and obedience under God's blessing, "if ye love me, keep my commandments" (John 14:15), the loving heart proving itself in the obedient walk, and finding life therein.

So the witnesses make the verse the way of life: The witnesses note the to love the LORD: the sum of the first table. The witnesses note the walk in his ways: the obedient walk. The witnesses note the keep his commandments: the spring of love. The witnesses note the life and blessing: if ye love me, keep my commandments (John 14:15). The expositors observe the to love the LORD: the sum of the first table. The expositors observe the walk in his ways: the obedient walk. The expositors observe the keep his commandments: the spring of love. The expositors observe the life and blessing: if ye love me, keep my commandments (John 14:15). The believer is taught to mark the to love the LORD, the walk in his ways, the keep his commandments, the life and blessing, and to choose life. The verse thus records that the way of life is to love God, walk in his ways, and keep his commandments — love the spring of obedience, the chosen walk under God's blessing, the loving heart keeping his commandments (John 14:15).
17:
"But if thine heart turn away, so that thou wilt not hear, but shalt be drawn away, and worship other gods, and serve them;" The witnesses note the way of death — the heart turning away to worship and serve other gods. The witnesses note that Gill notes the heart turning from the true God and his right worship, from his commands, statutes, and judgments. The witnesses note that they would be drawn away to serve other gods. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thine heart turn away — the inward defection; the wilt not hear — refusing God's voice; the drawn away — into idolatry; the death begins within — keep the heart with diligence (Prov. 4:23).

The witnesses agree the way of "death and evil" begins in the heart's turning: "if thine heart turn away... and worship other gods"; the root of apostasy is the inward turning of the heart, which then is "drawn away" into idolatry and false service; the verse traces the path of ruin from the secret defection of the heart to open idolatry; it teaches that the soul's departure from God starts within, the heart turning before the feet stray, and warns against the first inward declension, "keep thy heart with all diligence; for out of it are the issues of life" (Prov. 4:23), the turned heart the beginning of death.

So the witnesses make the verse the heart turns away: The witnesses note the thine heart turn away: the inward defection. The witnesses note the wilt not hear: refusing God's voice. The witnesses note the drawn away: into idolatry. The witnesses note the death begins within: keep the heart with diligence (Prov. 4:23). The expositors observe the thine heart turn away: the inward defection. The expositors observe the wilt not hear: refusing God's voice. The expositors observe the drawn away: into idolatry. The expositors observe the death begins within: keep the heart with diligence (Prov. 4:23). The believer is taught to mark the thine heart turn away, the wilt not hear, the drawn away, the death begins within, and to choose life. The verse thus records that the way of death is the heart turning away to serve other gods — apostasy beginning within, the heart turning before the feet stray, the warning to keep the heart with all diligence (Prov. 4:23).
18:
"I denounce unto you this day, that ye shall surely perish, and that ye shall not prolong your days upon the land..." The witnesses note the solemn denouncing of certain ruin upon those who turn from God. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses most solemnly averred this, that it would certainly be their case, by one judgment or another. The witnesses note that they should surely perish and not prolong their days. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I denounce this day — the solemn warning; the ye shall surely perish — certain ruin; the not prolong your days — the end of apostasy; the no idle threat — the soul that sinneth shall die (Ezek. 18:4).

The witnesses agree the alternative is sealed with a solemn warning of sure destruction: "I denounce unto you... that ye shall surely perish"; Moses presses the certainty of the doom on apostasy, that it might be feared and shunned; the verse sets the dreadful end of the way of death plainly before the people, that the choice be made with open eyes; it teaches that the wages of forsaking God is certain ruin, no idle threat but a sure denouncing, "the soul that sinneth, it shall die" (Ezek. 18:4), the end of the turning heart made plain that men might flee it.

So the witnesses make the verse ye shall surely perish: The witnesses note the I denounce this day: the solemn warning. The witnesses note the ye shall surely perish: certain ruin. The witnesses note the not prolong your days: the end of apostasy. The witnesses note the no idle threat: the soul that sinneth shall die (Ezek. 18:4). The expositors observe the I denounce this day: the solemn warning. The expositors observe the ye shall surely perish: certain ruin. The expositors observe the not prolong your days: the end of apostasy. The expositors observe the no idle threat: the soul that sinneth shall die (Ezek. 18:4). The believer is taught to mark the I denounce this day, the ye shall surely perish, the not prolong your days, the no idle threat, and to choose life. The verse thus records that Moses denounced certain ruin on those who turn away — the dreadful end of the way of death set plainly before them, the wages of forsaking God sure, the soul that sinneth shall die (Ezek. 18:4).
19:
"I call heaven and earth to record this day against you, that I have set before you life and death, blessing and cursing: therefore choose life, that both thou and thy seed may live:" The witnesses note the solemn appeal to heaven and earth, and the great charge — "choose life." The witnesses note that Gill notes the calling of heaven and earth as witnesses — either the inanimate bodies, or figuratively their inhabitants, angels and men. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set life and death, blessing and cursing, with the charge to choose life. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the call heaven and earth — the solemn witnesses; the life and death set — blessing and cursing; the choose life — the great charge; the the momentous choice — choose you whom ye will serve (Josh. 24:15).

The witnesses agree this verse rises to the height of the whole appeal, calling the universe to witness the choice set before them: "I call heaven and earth to record... therefore choose life"; the solemn invoking of witnesses marks the weight of the decision and the people's responsibility; and the great command — "choose life" — is the heart of it, God urging his creatures to take the way of blessing; it teaches that man is set in a place of real and momentous choice, summoned with all solemnity to choose God and life over sin and death, "choose you this day whom ye will serve" (Josh. 24:15), the charge "choose life" the call of God to every soul.

So the witnesses make the verse choose life: The witnesses note the call heaven and earth: the solemn witnesses. The witnesses note the life and death set: blessing and cursing. The witnesses note the choose life: the great charge. The witnesses note the the momentous choice: choose you whom ye will serve (Josh. 24:15). The expositors observe the call heaven and earth: the solemn witnesses. The expositors observe the life and death set: blessing and cursing. The expositors observe the choose life: the great charge. The expositors observe the the momentous choice: choose you whom ye will serve (Josh. 24:15). The believer is taught to mark the call heaven and earth, the life and death set, the choose life, the the momentous choice, and to choose life. The verse thus records that Moses called heaven and earth to witness and charged them, "choose life" — the height of the appeal, man summoned to the momentous choice of God and life over sin and death (Josh. 24:15).
20:
"That thou mayest love the LORD thy God, and that thou mayest obey his voice, and that thou mayest cleave unto him: for he is thy life, and the length of thy days..." The witnesses note the end of the choice — to love, obey, and cleave to God, who is himself their life. The witnesses note that Gill notes loving God and showing it by keeping his commands, obeying his voice in his word and by his prophets, and cleaving to him and his worship. The witnesses note that he is their life and the length of their days, the God of their lives. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the love the LORD — the end of the choice; the obey his voice — and cleave unto him; the he is thy life — the source of their days; the choosing life is choosing God — the true God and eternal life (1 John 5:20).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes by naming God himself as the life that is chosen: to "love... obey... and cleave unto him," "for he is thy life, and the length of thy days"; the choosing of life is, at bottom, the choosing of God, who is himself the life of his people, the source and length of their days; the verse teaches that true life is not a thing apart from God but found in him alone, the soul that cleaves to him having life, "this is the true God, and eternal life" (1 John 5:20); to choose life is to choose the LORD, in whom is "the fountain of life" (Ps. 36:9), and cleaving to him to have all.

So the witnesses make the verse he is thy life: The witnesses note the love the LORD: the end of the choice. The witnesses note the obey his voice: and cleave unto him. The witnesses note the he is thy life: the source of their days. The witnesses note the choosing life is choosing God: the true God and eternal life (1 John 5:20). The expositors observe the love the LORD: the end of the choice. The expositors observe the obey his voice: and cleave unto him. The expositors observe the he is thy life: the source of their days. The expositors observe the choosing life is choosing God: the true God and eternal life (1 John 5:20). The believer is taught to mark the love the LORD, the obey his voice, the he is thy life, the choosing life is choosing God, and to choose life. The verse thus records that they were to love, obey, and cleave to God, "for he is thy life" — the choosing of life at bottom the choosing of God himself, the fountain of life, the true God and eternal life (1 John 5:20; Ps. 36:9).
